name: Wormwood Absinthe
description: Wormwood (Artemisia absinthium), a bitter herb central to the creation of absinthe, holds a complex and often misunderstood place in European tradition. Beyond its psychoactive properties, stemming from the thujone content, it was believed to possess potent medicinal and magical powers. Historically, wormwood was used as a remedy for digestive ailments, intestinal parasites (hence the name), and as a febrifuge.  Its association with absinthe, popularized in 19th-century France, led to its demonization as a cause of madness and societal decay, fueling the 'green fairy' myth.  Before absinthe, wormwood had long been used in traditional herbal remedies and folk magic. It was believed to have protective properties, warding off evil spirits and promoting clairvoyance.  Burning wormwood was thought to cleanse spaces and banish negativity.  In some traditions, it was used in love potions and divination rituals.  The herb's bitter taste became symbolic of hardship, regret, and the darker aspects of human experience, contributing to its complex symbolism. The drink absinthe became a symbol of bohemianism, artistic expression, and rebellion, further cementing wormwood's place in European cultural lore. The ban on absinthe throughout much of the 20th century only enhanced its mystique, making it a forbidden and alluring elixir steeped in history and myth. Even today, wormwood retains an aura of mystery and is often associated with altered states of consciousness and the exploration of the subconscious. Its enduring legacy lies in its ability to inspire both fear and fascination, embodying the duality of nature's power.
subtitle: The Green Fairy's Bitter Kiss

keyMyths.title: The Green Fairy
keyMyths.description: Absinthe, containing wormwood, was personified as a 'Green Fairy' who inspired creativity and madness. This myth contributed to the drink's ban in the early 20th century.

sources.author: Phil Baker
sources.description: A comprehensive history and cultural exploration of absinthe.
